request.question: Londonderry Borough Police are the force in the county that is part of what country?

title: Reading Borough Police
Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920
St Ives Borough Police
County Londonderry
Penzance Borough Police
Law enforcement in Ireland
Newcastle upon Tyne City Police
Londonderry Borough Police

sentences: Reading Borough Police was a police force for the borough of Reading in the United Kingdom.

The force was created on 21 February 1836, at which time it had a strength of 30 constables, two sergeants and two inspectors. Towards the end of the 19th century, Reading Borough Police had increased in size to 62 officers.
However, the local population had risen to around 60,500, which meant one officer for every 1,000 inhabitants.
By the time of the First World War the force had an establishment of 113 officers, however, due to military service only 30 officers were patrolling Reading.
An election to Londonderry County Borough Council took place in January 1920 as part of that year's Irish local elections.
The Local Government (Ireland) Act 1919 had seen elections for local government in Ireland change to a more proportional system.
As a result Unionists lost control over Londonderry borough council, which for the first time passed into the control of the city's Catholic majority.
St Ives Borough Police was the borough police force for the town of St Ives, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1889.
It was established following the passage of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835.
The force was unique in that it only boasted a single constable at any one time throughout its entire existence.
Since 1972, the counties in Northern Ireland; including Londonderry; have no longer been used by the state as part of the local administration.
Following further reforms in 2015, the area is now governed under three different districts; Derry and Strabane, Causeway Coast and Glens and Mid-Ulster.
Despite no longer being used for local government and administrative purposes, it is sometimes used in a cultural context in All-Ireland sporting and cultural events (ie - Derry GAA).
Penzance Borough Police was the police force for the borough and corporate town of Penzance, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1947.
It was formed following the passing of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835, which reformed all UK boroughs, and stipulated that each appoint a Watch Committee to oversee a police force.
The police force formed part of the commonality of the town’s government, led by an elected Mayor, six aldermen and eighteen councillors.
This is a description of law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.

Before the Republic (then called the Irish Free State) left the union, one police force - the Royal Irish Constabulary - policed almost the whole island (aside from Dublin, where the Dublin Metropolitan Police were the main force, Belfast where the Belfast Borough Police were the main force and the borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC.
See List of defunct law enforcement agencies in the United Kingdom and Irish Police records and also Royal Irish Constabulary Forum for other forces in Ireland that have been disbanded.)
Newcastle upon Tyne City Police also known as Newcastle upon Tyne Borough Police, was the Home office Police force and a Constabulary that served the City of Newcastle upon Tyne with origins in 1836.
On 1 April 1969 it was amalgamated into the Northumberland Constabulary under the Police Act 1964.
The first Modern Police Force was formed in Newcastle in 1836, 3 years before the County Police Act of 1839.
The Londonderry Borough Police was the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870, nicknamed the Horney Dicks after the bones used in their helmets.
They replaced the earlier town watch and were in turn replaced by the Royal Irish Constabulary.
The police force was established by the Londonderry Improvement Act 1848 and were governed by the Londonderry Corporation.
Its abolition was recommended by a commission of inquiry into sectarian riots in the city in 1869.

The commission felt the force, having the form of a town watch, was inadequate to policing serious crime; it also noted, but did not endorse, allegations that the police discriminated against Roman Catholics.
Folkestone Borough Police was the borough police force for the town of Folkestone in Kent.
It was formed in 1851 under the terms of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835.
The Ferguson Baronetcy, of the City of Londonderry, was a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom.
It was created on 7 October 1801 for Andrew Ferguson, who had previously represented Londonderry Borough in the Irish House of Commons.
The second Baronet represented Londonderry in the British House of Commons.
The title became extinct on his death in 1860.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Londonderry Borough Police are the force in the county that is part of what country?
hop1_docs: Londonderry Borough Police | The Londonderry Borough Police was the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland from 1848 to 1870, nicknamed the Horney Dicks after the bones used in their helmets.  They replaced the earlier town watch and were in turn replaced by the Royal Irish Constabulary.  The police force was established by the Londonderry Improvement Act 1848 and were governed by the Londonderry Corporation.  Its abolition was recommended by a commission of inquiry into sectarian riots in the city in 1869.  The commission felt the force, having the form of a town watch, was inadequate to policing serious crime; it also noted, but did not endorse, allegations that the police discriminated against Roman Catholics.

Law enforcement in Ireland | This is a description of law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland.  Before the Republic (then called the Irish Free State) left the union, one police force - the Royal Irish Constabulary - policed almost the whole island (aside from Dublin, where the Dublin Metropolitan Police were the main force, Belfast where the Belfast Borough Police were the main force and the borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC.  See List of defunct law enforcement agencies in the United Kingdom and Irish Police records and also Royal Irish Constabulary Forum for other forces in Ireland that have been disbanded.) .

Londonderry County Borough Council election, 1920 | An election to Londonderry County Borough Council took place in January 1920 as part of that year's Irish local elections.  The Local Government (Ireland) Act 1919 had seen elections for local government in Ireland change to a more proportional system.  As a result Unionists lost control over Londonderry borough council, which for the first time passed into the control of the city's Catholic majority.

Barnsley Borough Police | Barnsley Borough Police was the police force operating in county borough of Barnsley, England until 1 October 1968.  The force was merged with other forces in 1968 to form the West Yorkshire Constabulary.  The area passed to the South Yorkshire Police in 1974.

Penzance Borough Police | Penzance Borough Police was the police force for the borough and corporate town of Penzance, Cornwall, from 1836 to 1947.  It was formed following the passing of the Municipal Corporations Act 1835, which reformed all UK boroughs, and stipulated that each appoint a Watch Committee to oversee a police force.  The police force formed part of the commonality of the town’s government, led by an elected Mayor, six aldermen and eighteen councillors.

Doncaster Borough Police | Doncaster Borough Police was the police force operating in the county borough of Doncaster, England until 1 October, 1968.  The force was then merged with others to form a West Yorkshire Constabulary.  From 1974 its area passed to the South Yorkshire Police.
Abingdon Borough Police | Abingdon Borough Police was the police force responsible for policing the Borough of Abingdon, situated in the county of Berkshire, England until 1889.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage [1] describes the force as "the police force in the city of Derry, County Londonderry, Ireland." Passage [2] places the topic in the context of "law enforcement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland" and refers to the "borough of Londonderry where the Londonderry Borough Police were the main force before merging with the RIC." Missing/next-hop: confirm which modern country County Londonderry is part of (i.e., whether County Londonderry is in Northern Ireland / the United Kingdom or in the Republic of Ireland). Retrieval direction/anchors: look up "County Londonderry country", "County Londonderry is in Northern Ireland", or the article "County Londonderry" (aka County Derry) to get its current national affiliation.
response.hop2_query: County Londonderry country Northern Ireland United Kingdom
hop2_docs: Macosquin Abbey | Macosquin Abbey formally known as "Clarus Fons "was a Cistercian Monastery in County Londonderry, Northern Ireland in the United Kingdom.  The Monastery was located on Abbey Lane, Macosquin, Northern Ireland.

Northern Regional College | Northern Regional College (or NRC) is a third level educational institution in Northern Ireland, United Kingdom.  The college has seven campuses around the north-east of Northern Ireland (County Antrim and eastern County Londonderry); Ballymena, Ballymoney, Coleraine, Felden, Larne, Magherafelt and Newtownabbey.  It also had a campus in Antrim but the Antrim campus has since closed

Roe Valley Country Park | The Roe Valley Country Park is a forested area containing part of the River Roe, south west of Limavady, County Londonderry, Northern Ireland.  It is maintained by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, which is part of the Department of the Environment of Northern Ireland.

McFarland baronets | The McFarland Baronetcy, of Aberfoyle in the County of Londonderry, is a title in the Baronetage of the United Kingdom.  It was created on 23 January 1914 for the Irish businessman John McFarland.  He was founder of and a partner in the firm of McCrea and McFarland, engineering contractors, of Belfast and Derry, Chairman of Mulhollands, drapers, and owner of the Lough Swilly Steamship Company, and also served as Mayor of Londonderry.  He was succeeded by his son, the second Baronet.  He was a businessman, a Senator of Northern Ireland, Mayor of Londonderry, Lord Lieutenant of the City of Londonderry and an Irish Rugby union international.  The third Baronet has been a member of the Londonderry Corporation and a Deputy Lieutenant of the City of the County of Londonderry from 1956 to 1982.

Foyle College | Foyle College is a co-educational non-denominational voluntary grammar school in Derry City, County Londonderry, Northern Ireland.  The school's legal name is Foyle and Londonderry College.  In 1976, two local schools, Foyle College and Londonderry High School, merged under the Foyle and Londonderry College Act 1976 to form Foyle and Londonderry College.  In 2011, the Board of Governors re-branded the school as 'Foyle College' and updated the school's crest.  The school is a member of the Independent Schools Council of the United Kingdom and also the Headmasters' and Headmistresses' Conference (HMC).

Causeway Institute | The Causeway Institute of Further and Higher Education (informally Causeway Institute or CIFHE) was a third-level educational institution in Northern Ireland, United Kingdom.  The Causeway Institute was located on two campuses: Coleraine, County Londonderry and Ballymoney, County Antrim.

List of places in County Londonderry | This is a list of cities, towns, villages, parishes and hamlets in County Londonderry, Northern Ireland.  See the list of places in Northern Ireland for places in other counties.
hop2_titles: List of places in County Londonderry
response.summary_2: The retrieved passages contradict the context's unqualified "Ireland" phrasing: County Londonderry is in Northern Ireland, part of the United Kingdom (e.g., passages [1], [3], [5]).
